Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd kind of drift through the week and then suddenly realize I had stuff due. When I did study, I'd usually do it in bursts—maybe watch some videos or skim through materials, but it was never consistent. I know I should have been more organized, but I just couldn't find the motivation to stick to a schedule.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, really. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, and then when deadlines hit, I'd scramble to catch up. It wasn't intentional—I just kept getting distracted by other things, my extracurriculars, hanging out with friends. The anxiety would build up, but somehow I'd still wait until the last minute.
Q3: Usually it was panic, to be honest. I'd get a notification about a deadline or remember that I hadn't checked in for a while, and that would push me to log in. Sometimes I'd log in just to see what was happening, scroll around a bit, but not really do anything productive. It was more about checking than actually engaging.
Q4: There were a few weeks where I got into a rhythm of logging in every couple of days, mostly because I was following some discussion threads that were actually interesting. But it never really stuck as a habit. Something would come up—a club event, stress from other stuff—and I'd fall off again. It felt more accidental than intentional when I was consistent.
Q5: The videos were definitely the most useful for me since I'm a visual learner. I could watch them at my own pace, pause, rewind when something didn't click. Some of the diagrams and visual materials really helped concepts stick in a way that reading never did. I probably spent most of my productive LMS time on those.
Q6: The reading materials and some of the discussion prompts—I pretty much ignored those. Reading long texts just doesn't work for my brain, and the discussion posts felt forced sometimes. I'd skim them if I had to, but I rarely engaged deeply. It felt like busywork when I was already stressed about other things.
Q7: If I could see how it connected to the exam or if it was genuinely interesting, I'd put in the effort. Activities that felt like they'd actually help me understand something—not just check a box—those were worth it. But if something felt repetitive or disconnected from what I needed to know, I'd skip it without much guilt.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff felt completely different. Required activities had this weight to them, even if I procrastinated on them. Optional things? I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later never came. It's weird because some optional stuff was probably more valuable, but without that external pressure, I just let it slide.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download slides and videos and study from my laptop without being connected to the LMS. I also used YouTube a lot to find alternative explanations when the course material wasn't clicking. My friends and I would sometimes study together in person, working through problems on whiteboards. None of that showed up anywhere online.
Q10: There were times I'd spend hours going over downloaded notes or working through practice problems on paper. I'd be genuinely learning, but my LMS would show nothing because I wasn't logged in. Or I'd watch a video once online, then replay it from my downloads multiple times. The system only saw that one view.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture video or PDF, watch or read it once while logged in, and then keep coming back to it offline for days. My actual engagement with that material was way higher than what the system recorded. It was just more convenient to have everything saved locally.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got harder. I had a lot going on with my extracurriculars, and the anxiety was really peaking. I'd open the LMS and just feel overwhelmed by how much I'd fallen behind. The mindfulness exercises they suggested helped a bit, but some days I just couldn't face it. I'd close my laptop and do something else to cope.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted or stressed, the LMS felt like this mountain I couldn't climb. I'd log in with good intentions, but then I'd see all the tasks piling up and just freeze. Competing responsibilities—especially my extracurriculars—meant something always had to give, and unfortunately it was often the coursework.
Q14: Yeah, there were plenty of times I'd be scrolling through materials but my brain was somewhere else entirely. I'd have a video playing but I'd be thinking about something stressful or just zoning out. Technically I was "engaging" with the content, but I wasn't absorbing anything. It was more like going through the motions.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they mattered. The anxiety made it worse—I'd feel stressed about starting, so I'd avoid it, which made me more stressed. It's this terrible cycle. I knew the assignments were important, especially since my scores were actually good when I did them, but getting started was the hardest part.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me act. Without them, I'd drift indefinitely. When a deadline was close, I'd suddenly become hyper-focused and get things done. It wasn't healthy, but it worked enough to get me through. The LMS activity probably spiked right before every due date.
Q17: When I got good feedback, it surprised me and actually motivated me a bit. Like, seeing that my assignment scores were high made me think maybe I wasn't as hopeless as I felt. But that motivation was temporary. I'd feel good for a day, then slip back into my usual patterns. Feedback helped, but it didn't fundamentally change how I operated.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which was both good and bad for someone like me. Good because I could work at my own pace, bad because I need external structure to stay on track. When things were spread out, I'd procrastinate. When modules were more contained with clear deadlines, I did better.
Q19: Having videos and visual content made engagement easier for sure. But the sheer number of resources sometimes made it harder—I didn't know where to start, so I'd just not start at all. Clearer guidance on what was essential versus supplementary would have helped me prioritize instead of feeling paralyzed.
Q20: That sounds like me, honestly. I'd log in a lot, watch videos, browse around, but then not actually submit the work. Sometimes it's anxiety about the assignment itself, or feeling like what you produce won't be good enough. You're engaging with the learning, but the final step of submitting feels too high-stakes.
Q21: That also sounds like me in a way. My exam scores were decent because I'm actually pretty good at understanding material when I focus, even if my attendance and submissions were terrible. I studied a lot offline, crammed effectively before exams. The LMS didn't capture that I was learning—just that I wasn't showing up consistently.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you log in, what you click on, how long you spend. But they miss the quality of that time. They can't tell if you're actually thinking or just staring at the screen. They definitely miss offline studying, group work, and the mental state you're in when you're engaging.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting mental effort into understanding it. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials. For me, real engagement is when I'm curious, when I'm connecting ideas, when I actually want to figure something out—not just when I'm trying to survive a deadline.
Q24: The anxiety and the internal struggle—none of that shows up in data. The times I wanted to engage but couldn't because I was overwhelmed, the offline studying, the conversations with friends where we worked through concepts together. My resilience in bouncing back after rough patches. The data just sees surface-level actions, not what's happening underneath.
